Board-Sourced Licensing Intelligence

Dietitian License
State Directory

Stop decoding state board websites. Find your state's 2026 dietitian licensing requirements, fees, board contacts, and step-by-step application roadmaps.

📅 Updated weekly · Last verified: February 2026 · 🏛 Board sources cited per state
Timeline
Fee
✅ What this is: We provide the operational intelligence—board-sourced, statute-aligned roadmaps—so you submit a clean application on the first try.
🚫 Not a filing service. We don't file your application for you.

Alabama

Licensed Dietitian (LD)
2 to 4 Weeks · $250

Alaska

Licensed Dietitian
4 to 6 Weeks · $150

Arizona

Registered Dietitian (RD/RDN) — No State License Required
N/A (No state application) · $0 (State Fee)

Arkansas

Licensed Dietitian (LD)
2 to 4 Weeks · $110

California

Registered Dietitian (RD)
Not Applicable · $0

Colorado

Licensed Dietitian (LD) — Effective September 1, 2026
TBD (New program launching Sept 2026) · ~$239.50 (Estimated)

Connecticut

Certified Dietitian-Nutritionist (CDN)
4 to 6 Weeks · $190

Delaware

Licensed Dietitian/Nutritionist (LDN)
3 to 5 Weeks · $235

District of Columbia

Licensed Dietitian
~3–5 Weeks · $229

Florida

Licensed Dietitian/Nutritionist
30 days (initial review) · $165

Georgia

Licensed Dietitian (LD)
15 to 20 Business Days · $85

Licensed Dietitian (LD)
4 to 6 Weeks · $225

Licensed Dietitian (LD)
3 to 4 Weeks · $100

Licensed Dietitian Nutritionist (LDN)
4 to 6 Weeks · $100

2 to 4 Weeks · $20

Don’t let bad paperwork delay your first paycheck.

Select your target state to see board-sourced requirements, exact fees, and a step-by-step application roadmap.

State Licensing Reference

Independent, YMYL-compliant licensing guides for dietitians and nutritionists across the United States.

Disclaimer: State Licensing Reference is an independent software tool and workflow resource. We are not a law firm, and we are not affiliated with any state or federal dietetics licensing board. While our roadmaps are board-sourced and statute-aligned, state laws change frequently. Users must always verify final regulatory requirements directly with their specific state board prior to submitting applications or fees.
